## Thread: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system" — 122 this month / 141 total

_Thread started [2007-09](2007-09.md#t-be0407e98e)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-10-07T21:48:34-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u8vig35ar5mga89e1p4228gk0dontjuf4j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <qMGLi.150556$HU1.29309@fe05.news.easynews.com> <5m989bFceagcU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sun, 30 Sep 2007 22:23:53 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Indexed files, without manual intervention.

Interestingly, this problem surfaced with the PC and Unix boxes which
do not have an indexed file system as a part of the operating system
unlike the operating systems associated with the business computers
from the various manufacturers.  While conversion to RDB's is probably
the best way to go on the PC and UNIX systems, I would hope that a
data analyst and data base analyst is involved so that it isn't
putting each indexed file complete with multiple record formats into
a table that matches the indexed file.  That way you get the increased
overhead of the RDB without any of the benefits.
>
>I think most sites have converted to RDB long ago (it is quite a painful 
>manual process), but this will help with the ones who haven't... :-)
>
>Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-08T15:55:47+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mtkhjFelt42U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <qMGLi.150556$HU1.29309@fe05.news.easynews.com> <5m989bFceagcU1@mid.individual.net> <u8vig35ar5mga89e1p4228gk0dontjuf4j@4ax.com>`

```


"Clark F Morris" <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:u8vig35ar5mga89e1p4228gk0dontjuf4j@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 30 Sep 2007 22:23:53 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> overhead of the RDB without any of the benefits.

No, the tool set is smarter than that. The end point is a DB in 2 or 3 NF. 
(Usually it is 3NF but it cannot be always.)

(I researched, designed, and wrote it over several years (it wasn't 
something dashed off in 5 minutes, addressing only the obvious :-)); the 
latest release is simply the last incarnation of it, where I managed to take 
most of the existing COBOL components and convert them to COM servers (if 
they weren't already), then wrap them with Interop Services so they can be 
controlled from C#. At the same time, some new functionality has been added 
in C# and the User interface is now DotNET/Vista, rather than the clunky 
Windows interface provided with PowerCOBOL.  All of the tools have been 
integrated, and run from a single tabbed "Management Console"   I am 
currently polishing it and adding some "added value" features. It looks 
great and behaves very acceptably :-))

But even if this weren't so, there are STILL benefits in moving to a  RDBMS.

1. The data is now accessible remotely and locally with standard tools.
2. The data can be sorted, sliced, diced and served WITHOUT having to write 
a program to do so.

In my opinion, even a straight "Record/Table" equivalence, as you describe 
would be of more overall value to the organization than retaining "closed" 
Indexed files.

(It also provides a starting point for incremental redesign...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T02:55:23+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mcd3qFba85sU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <81fuf356bta6c1gb61rae7d79jb92rhvh5@4ax.com> <1191179230.655139.4510@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <5maeb4Fcd7fuU1@mid.individual.net> <1191195795.978155.209550@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```


"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1191195795.978155.209550@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> On Oct 1, 8:13 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah, clear. Thanks.

Yes, after migration, code conversion is the next major effort. I'm hoping 
to help them automate some (maybe even most...) of that too. There are a few 
hundred COBOL programs and, unfortunately, it isn't just a simple matter of 
converting Indexed access verbs to SQL. There are many implications with 
group fields defined in COBOL, different handling of Date types, OCCURS and 
REDEFINES.  It is pretty huge. I've just finished writing a 20 page document 
that identifies the problem areas, looks at what could be automated and how, 
and examines possible strategies to accomplish the code conversion.

For example, the existing code references various group fields, but the RDB 
cannot support group fields.(It has to be stored as a separate element on 
the DB, or could be saved as a View) It may be possible to generate a stored 
procedure that will automatically update the group field whenever one of the 
constituent fields changes, but the ideal thing would be to drop references 
to group fields and replace them with SQL. It really depends on how much 
referencing there is and how process critical it is.

Initially, we will want to make the minimum changes to the existing COBOL 
code, but we may have no choice as work progresses. This is another reason 
why we may convert subsytems at a time rather than doing all the data 
migration then all the code conversion.

At least they are staying with COBOL, in the meantime, anyway :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Tue, 2 Oct 2007 02:55:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
>referencing there is and how process critical it is.

Why should this be a problem?  The RDB would store the individual
fields but when setting up the view the individual fields could still
effectively make up the group field so that when the row is read into
working storage there would be a redefines that would have the group
and individual fields.  If the key is currently a group field, SQL
concatenation can handle that.  I learned that in fixing an
application converted from mainframe.  I won't go into how many
mistakes the people who converted it made. 
>
>Initially, we will want to make the minimum changes to the existing COBOL 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-08T16:20:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mtlvkFfbgodU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <81fuf356bta6c1gb61rae7d79jb92rhvh5@4ax.com> <1191179230.655139.4510@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <5maeb4Fcd7fuU1@mid.individual.net> <1191195795.978155.209550@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <5mcd3qFba85sU1@mid.individual.net> <huvig3peviqn6655468o2g4ssc351a6i3d@4ax.com>`

```


"Clark F Morris" <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:huvig3peviqn6655468o2g4ssc351a6i3d@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 2 Oct 2007 02:55:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[120 more quoted lines elided]…
> and individual fields.

Isn't that what I said above? ("...or could be saved as a View"). You have 
simply assumed using View as a solution; I have other options.

You assume a "row" will be read into WS and that just perpetuates record 
rather than relational processing.

The toolset does include options to allow such processing (it generates 
"record" access modules also), but these are intended as conversion aids 
only, to be replaced by better relational processing.

And working-storage does not need to be a redefinition; a group level can do 
it.

To manually define Views for hundreds of groups  of two to six elements, is 
not something I would do myself and I therefore don't expect other people to 
do it. Apart from the awful overheads this incurs, it means that the "right" 
view has to be specified every time you access what was once a group field. 
While that COULD possibly be generated into code conversion tools, I 
wouldn't be jumping up and down with enthusiasm to write it... There is 
considerable work in it. (It is difficult enough accessing schemas to get 
table and element definitions, without doing it for hundreds of Views as 
well :-))

There are times when this would be acceptable, and there are times when it 
isn't. Hence, the careful wording of my paragraph above. Using views, for 
me, is a POSSIBLE solution. It may be part of other solutions; I don't see 
it as the "only way".

Pete.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:c9mvf3hoqs5j2fcdeqf3iq5dic1mq4tq1i@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 30 Sep 2007 21:29:31 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> If that's all you want to do, you don't need remote compilation.

I am sure there are many ways to achieve what I want. But that is not "all I 
want to do" (This is only one aspect of a toolset that dleivers many things)

If COBOL is not running on the machine where I want to run the output, then 
it makes sense to access it remotely.

Besides, there may be many reasons I would want to compile COBOL from any 
machine on the network, without buying additional COBOL licences.
>
> 1.  Have your program do the copybook interpretation at execution time, 
> build the SQL
> dynamically.



>One compilation would handle all 100 ISAM files. I posted such a program
> here. It converted any ISAM (with copybook) to comma delimited (CSV), 
> which could then be
> loaded into a spreadsheet or database.

Yes, I already have software to do that, it's trivial. That is not the 
problem. I need to analyse the COPYBOOK and generate DDL (NOT SQL) for any 
one of several target RDBMS systems. This creates tables on the database and 
these are normalized to 3NF, repeating groups removed, key dependencies 
checked and combined. It is a lot more than simply transferring data from 
ISAM to a replicated structure.

>
> 2. Fujitsu shipped a tool that converts ISAM to flat file. Run that tool 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> already a flat file.

And how would I get the needed DB structure from doing that?
>
> 3. Transmit (FTP) the ISAM file/index and do the conversion locally.

No idea what you are talking about here. The conversion IS done locally 
using a single integrated toolset. That toolset obtains data and compiles 
COBOL whenever it needs to, and does it locally or remotely, transparently 
to the user.

>
> 4. Mount the remote directory on your file system and do the conversion 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> functionality.

I think you have completely misunderstood what is going on here. You keep 
saying "do the conversion locally". It isn't about "conversion". It is about 
creating a database and loading it. It may be done one or more files at a 
time. The existing COBOL access code also has to be converted and they may 
want to do that on a subsystem basis. They need ot be able to dynamically 
modify their copybooks and instantly regenerate and load the affected 
tables. There is much more to it than I have described here.
>
> 5. Use an ODBC driver from http://www.transoft.com/products/ts_sqlgate.htm 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> ideas as the enemy.

I appreciate the imaginative solutions siggested, Robert, but you do not 
have a complete problem definition here.

Pete.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:1aa0g3dkekdlcrv26f6vbu041l03m7snoj@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 1 Oct 2007 09:34:59 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> automate detection of transitive dependencies to get 3NF?

You can't always... However, what I have does achieve it most of the time.
>
>>> 2. Fujitsu shipped a tool that converts ISAM to flat file. Run that tool
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It does have a facility to allow people with no SQL knowledge to access the 
DB, using COBOL record layouts. It is based around a data layer that is part 
of an n-tier architecture and COBOL simply calls the bottom layer. (Access 
modules for each table are generated the same way that Load modules are, 
except that the access modules don't read ISAM; they provide Select, Insert, 
Change, and Delete functionality, instead. This is intended as a an aid 
while people are learning SQL, I certainly don't want to encourage continued 
"record" based processing. There won't be any 10 way joins ... :-)

Pete.
```

```
Well, actually, I did want an answer to that but only in passing :-) I meant 
it more as an example.

Nevertheless, I od appreciate the responses received from all who posted. It 
has been very helpful.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2007-10-01T10:47:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net...

It was precisely because I have no easy way to access the ISAM from anything
> OTHER THAN COBOL that I had to go for a COBOL generation, and that in
turn,
> made me think about the "illusion" of cross platform standardization that
> COBOL is supposed to provide...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the mid-80s because the corporate data resource was (apparently) locked
into
> COBOL and this therefore guaranteed COBOL's future. (I wonder where they
are
> now...)
>


It's a bit much, isn't it, to blame the language for the differing ways that
implementors chose to write their I/O handlers?    I don't know that the
standards ever specified how the files should be implemented.  For that
matter, how could they have done so?  At the very minimum, they'd have had
cope with EBCDIC vs. ASCII, and count-key-data vs. fixed address disk
drives.  And that was long before PC days.

Is there any file system or DB system - any file mechanism whatsoever  that
is completely platform-independent?

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T12:50:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga>`

```


"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga...
>
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementors chose to write their I/O handlers?

I'm not blaming COBOL, that would be stupid.

In fact, blaming anybody for anything is a pretty fruitless exercise; it 
changes nothing and simply demotivates everyone. When things go wrong there 
are two things that must happen:

1. The person or persons responsible must take responsibility and do 
whatever they can to fix it. (Usually this works best when they get the 
support of everyone else, and assigning blame or castigating them is not 
conducive to that process.)

2. The lessons as to what went wrong, and why, should be absorbed by all 
concerned, so that mistakes are not repeated.

>   I don't know that the
> standards ever specified how the files should be implemented.

I would hope that they didn't.

Might be better if we leave the Standards out of this. I see that as an even 
more contributory factor to the decline of COBOL than a closed file 
system... :-)

>For that
> matter, how could they have done so?  At the very minimum, they'd have had
> cope with EBCDIC vs. ASCII, and count-key-data vs. fixed address disk
> drives.  And that was long before PC days.

Yes, in its time, COBOL made an effort to cross platform boundaries and 
succeeded to the extent that you could compile mainly the same code on 
different platforms and it would run.

My point was that some programmers LIKED having everything in a closed file 
system where a COBOL program had to be written in order to access it. They 
short-sightedly supposed that this would give them job security for a long 
time to come. The same people resisted the introduction of open platforms 
like RDB, for the same reason. I have not forgotten the scorn and 
villification I received in a predecessor of this very forum, when I 
suggested that moving to RDB might be a good thing.  The world voted with 
its feet; they were not going to write COBOL programs to access data if they 
didn't HAVE to... COBOL was left behind.
>
> Is there any file system or DB system - any file mechanism whatsoever 
> that
> is completely platform-independent?

You're really not trying, Peter :-)

1. Text files. (unicode)
2. HTML files
3. CSV files
4. XML files
5. Service Data Objects (SDO)
6. Any RDBMS (These are open storage systems in the sense that they can be 
accessed locally and remotely, across platforms, without needing to write 
program code to do so. Object brokerage and facilities like ODBC are layers 
that make this possible.)

I could add half a dozen audio and another dozen video formats that are 
platform independent, inasmuch as they will run on any platform that 
supports them (You can't play TV on your mainframe, not because the file 
formats are incompatible, but because there is no hardware support.)

You asked for 1, there's two dozen...:-)

In fact, ANY file system that contains metadata which describes itself, can 
be platform independent.

Pete.
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> program code to do so. Object brokerage and facilities like ODBC are
layers

Yeah, well, I realized about eleven seconds too late what I'd said.  It was
meant to be a rhetorical statement that physical files on different
platforms aren't usually readable on another.  Tsk.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** bill@triangle.cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon)
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T12:43:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net>,
	"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> 
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> different platforms and it would run.

Still true, if you stick to COBOL and leave off the proprietary extensions.
But then, other languages suffer the same.

> 
> My point was that some programmers LIKED having everything in a closed file 
> system where a COBOL program had to be written in order to access it. They 
> short-sightedly supposed that this would give them job security for a long 
> time to come. 

It's been a long time since I was a full-time COBOL programmer (and I
really wish I still was or could be one again) but I never knew anyone
for whom that was motivation.  And I worked on a number of architectural
migrations involving COBOL.

>               The same people resisted the introduction of open platforms 
> like RDB, for the same reason. I have not forgotten the scorn and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> didn't HAVE to... COBOL was left behind.

I keep hearing this, but experience proves otherwise.  I have lietened 
to someone locally talking about how a local insurance company (think
blimp & beagle) is abandoning COBOL in favor of Java.  I have spoken
with the people who do the COBOL and they just laugh.

>>
>> Is there any file system or DB system - any file mechanism whatsoever 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 5. Service Data Objects (SDO)

Sorry, but none of these is truly platform-independant.  Not even text
files.  Ever try moving a text file from VMS to Unix (as a binary as
moving as a text will result in automatic conversion) and then editing
it with something like vi?  Even DOS (and I a fairly certain current
implementations of FAT) had more than one "type" of text file which
coulf not be edited by editors that did not know what the difference
was and how to deal with it.

> 6. Any RDBMS (These are open storage systems in the sense that they can be 
> accessed locally and remotely, across platforms, without needing to write 
> program code to do so. Object brokerage and facilities like ODBC are layers 
> that make this possible.)

Actually, they just provide the wedge in the form of some ?DBC.  You
may not have top write special code to handle it, but someone did.

> 
> I could add half a dozen audio and another dozen video formats that are 
> platform independent, inasmuch as they will run on any platform that 
> supports them (You can't play TV on your mainframe, not because the file 
> formats are incompatible, but because there is no hardware support.)

While the internal format of the data may be common and platform
independant, the files they are stored in are not guaranteed to
be so.  I need merely point out VMS again.  At the user level all
files are wrapped in RMS and it is very proprietary.

> 
> You asked for 1, there's two dozen...:-)
> 
> In fact, ANY file system that contains metadata which describes itself, can 
> be platform independent.

Funny, I would have said the exact opposite.  Unix files, which have no
meta-data and are merely streams of bytes seem the most independant.
VMS which always has meta-data the least.

bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T08:54:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On 2 Oct 2007 12:43:15 GMT, bill@triangle.cs.uofs.edu (Bill
Gunshannon) wrote:

>>               The same people resisted the introduction of open platforms 
>> like RDB, for the same reason. I have not forgotten the scorn and 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the people who do the COBOL and they just laugh

It wasn't too long ago when most everybody here thought CoBOL would
outlast Java.

Besides features of the languages, there are other reasons for
businesses to keep their old language, or to go to a new language:

- Most companies have or want packaged software as part of their IS.
When they maintain those packages, they will want to have workers who
are skilled in whatever languages those packages are written in.

- Most companies have expanded their IS to include Web features. Often
they have mainframe programmers and web programmers.    I haven't come
across any mainframe shops using CoBOL for their web apps.

- Companies like to hire programmers with experience programming in a
particular language.   Anybody can acquire experience using Java at no
great expense.  And the projects they do can be easily demonstrated.

-  Companies are moving towards centralized databases and data
warehouses.    The new cool tools that make it easy to use those are
often web based - at least those tools that the decision makers use
are.

- Companies get bought out by other companies.   This often means that
one company's IS gets taken over, and their data get combined.   The
easiest way to do this is by using standard databases and distributed
systems.

- Or my company's not changing, don't risk changing the IS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** bill@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon)

- **Message-ID:** `<5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com>,
	Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> On 2 Oct 2007 12:43:15 GMT, bill@triangle.cs.uofs.edu (Bill
> Gunshannon) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> outlast Java.

You talk like it hasn't.  Still lot's of COBOL around and it shows no
signs of leaving anytime soon.

> 
> Besides features of the languages, there are other reasons for
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> across any mainframe shops using CoBOL for their web apps.

I have seen some web apps written directly in COBOL and am looking
into doing some myself (just for fun at the moment but I do hope
to eventually get back into COBOL fulltime. Hint. Hint.)  But there
are a lot of sites where things are three tiered.  DB in the backend,
WEB Frontend and COBOL doin g the real work in the middle.  Probably
a good idea as one should always use the right tool for any job.

> 
> - Companies like to hire programmers with experience programming in a
> particular language.   Anybody can acquire experience using Java at no
> great expense.  And the projects they do can be easily demonstrated.

Maybe, but is the language truly suited for the tasks that are done
now using COBOL?  Much COBOL is still being done on IBM under CICS.
Somehow I can't imagine Java in this environment.

> 
> -  Companies are moving towards centralized databases and data
> warehouses.    The new cool tools that make it easy to use those are
> often web based - at least those tools that the decision makers use
> are.

But the web stuff mayonly be the frontend.  At least, it should be.

> 
> - Companies get bought out by other companies.   This often means that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Or my company's not changing, don't risk changing the IS.

I have seen a number of white-papers recently pointing out the idea
that COBOL is not likely to go away anytime soon and it's use may,
in fact, be growing still.  I, for one, am not planning on abandoning
my COBOL skills just yet.

bill
```

```
On 2 Oct 2007 17:27:01 GMT, bill@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon) wrote:

>> Besides features of the languages, there are other reasons for
>> businesses to keep their old language, or to go to a new language:
...

>Maybe, but is the language truly suited for the tasks that are done
>now using COBOL?  Much COBOL is still being done on IBM under CICS.
>Somehow I can't imagine Java in this environment.

Yes, but I was giving reasons that aren't related to features of the
languages.   Features that managers might be more comfortable
evaluating.     Don't count on upper management to evaluate things by
the same criteria that you use.

...

>I have seen a number of white-papers recently pointing out the idea
>that COBOL is not likely to go away anytime soon and it's use may,
>in fact, be growing still.  I, for one, am not planning on abandoning
>my COBOL skills just yet.

CoBOL is a native language for me - it's too ingrained for me to
abandon those skills.     But I'm preparing myself for alternatives.

Anybody much younger than myself would be foolish to count on his
CoBOL skills to last through retirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T15:19:34+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net>`

```


"Bill Gunshannon" <bill@cs.uofs.edu> wrote in message 
news:5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net...
> In article <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com>,
> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> writes:
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Fair enough. Your opinion is as good as anyone else's, including mine :-)

It's pretty easy to check the "growth" and popularity of COBOL vs the 
alternatives. Try GOOGLing on "COBOL Program", "VB program"  "JAVA program", 
"C# program" and "Ruby on Rails program".  Check the hit count. Doesn't 
prove anything, but certainly gives an indication. Then check any web based 
job site and try searching with COBOL as a required skill and the other 
languages mentioned. This is the REAL world, not the hallowed halls of 
Acadaemia, where White Papers shape opinions.

You may have missed a paradigm shift, Bill. The world moved to OO and the 
web. Even though COBOL CAN be used for these thngs (I have been writing OO 
COBOL for almost 10 years now and I have used COBOL to good effect with CGI 
and ISAPI on web sites), it suffers by comparison to the other tools 
available. There is no comparison between COBOL and C# or Java when it comes 
to web programming, or even application programming that is component based 
(and these days, nearly everything is...)

One question: When you say "COBOL" do you include OO COBOL?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** bill@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon)
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T12:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mhh7dFdjddnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net>,
	"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> 
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
> "C# program" and "Ruby on Rails program".  Check the hit count. 

Well, I can think of a couple reasons why I wouldn't place much value
on that metric.  Much of the COBOL use is not directly related to the
web.  It is in areas where the web plays such a small part I would not
expect to see a lot of hits.  The programmers are stable in their
positions so you don't see a lot of movement.  And then, the OS that is
most likely to be running COBOL is not used for web based applications,
CICS.  But even more importaqnt is the fact that Google sold their
integrity.  Just because Java rates higher than COBOL doesn't mean there
is more Java (don't get me wrong, I know there is, but that doesn't change
the fact that Google sells their ratings and therefore no Google search
can be considered a valid measure of popularity.)

>                                                                 Doesn't 
> prove anything, but certainly gives an indication. Then check any web based 
> job site and try searching with COBOL as a required skill and the other 
> languages mentioned. 

Actually, I have.  As I mentioned I would like to go back to
being a COBOL programmer full-time.  I found lots of COBOL
jobs.  Unfortunately, they fell into two classes.  The true
COBOL jobs were all CICS and not having worked in an IBM
environment for over 27 years my skills in that area are a
tad weak.  And the others were COBOL with {Java|Python|PHP|C#|.Net}
and we all know that means more of the latter than the former as
the former is probably pretty stable while the latter would
require constant modification.

>                      This is the REAL world, not the hallowed halls of 
> Acadaemia, where White Papers shape opinions.

Not all white-papers come out of academia.  A lot come out of people
like Gartner and, like it or not, they influence business.

> 
> You may have missed a paradigm shift, Bill. The world moved to OO and the 
> web. 

You may have missed the outcome of a major OO conference about
two years ago when the biggest "experts" in OO all came to the
conclusion that maybe the emperor really isn't wearing any clothes
after all. (The report from the conference is bound to still be on
the web somewhere)


>      Even though COBOL CAN be used for these thngs 

Yes, that's right, COBOL "can" be used for these things, but then,
that's not what COBOL was designed for and guess what, people are
still doing the thngs COBOL was designed for.

>                                                    (I have been writing OO 
> COBOL for almost 10 years now and I have used COBOL to good effect with CGI 
> and ISAPI on web sites), it suffers by comparison to the other tools 
> available.

Could that be because those are not the things COBOL was designed for?

>            There is no comparison between COBOL and C# or Java when it comes 
> to web programming, or even application programming that is component based 
> (and these days, nearly everything is...)

The financial industry would likely disagree with you there.

> 
> One question: When you say "COBOL" do you include OO COBOL?

I know there is OO COBOL, but most of the things that COBOL is good
at are not OO based.  Contrary to recent popular belief, the whole
world is not made up of "Objects".  The OO paradigm is not the be all,
do all of the computing industry.  And, if you search for and find
the report I mentioned above, you will find that even the OO community
has come to that realization.

bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T19:55:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p0c8g3986h32mqpsbeihodbo89oasek6um@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <5mhh7dFdjddnU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On 3 Oct 2007 12:45:34 GMT, bill@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon) wrote:

>Actually, I have.  As I mentioned I would like to go back to
>being a COBOL programmer full-time.  I found lots of COBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>require constant modification.

I've been a pure Cobol on Unix contractor for 9+ years. No mainframe and no Java/Web. Half
the shops are trying to pretend the Unix box is a mainframe. They have mainframe style
programming standards and lousy code, run scripts that look like JCL. The other half are
full of C programmers 'stuck' with maintaining Cobol. Their code quality and general
skill level is much higher than the first type. Both do batch processing. Interactive apps
are done by a separate Java group that you'll probably never meet.

Additional skills required in the Unix world are SQL and shell scripting. In the second
type of shop you also need C, PERL and PL/SQL. In the first type, Powerbuilder and other
obsolete tools are still in use, but experience with them is not required. 

Mainframe style Unix is found in small to medium sized companies and government agencies.
Unix style Unix is found in very large F-100 companies.
```

```
experience but you would be able to answer some interview questions. It can 
be hard to write practice CICS programs and play with them as it often

work.  Environments vary however a nd some places may allow programmers to 
do these kinds of things in a test or sandbox environment.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:dptcg3djgstirp8e7cv1gpka0ca3ub0tq0@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 05 Oct 2007 08:57:36 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> contractors who had worked at both.

Let me ASSURE you that a significant portion of IBM's income comes from 
mainframes AND mainframe software sold to US (and local) government entities. 
This is not just a "mature" market for them, but one that show steady growth in 
sales (and profits).  There are usually a NUMBER of US government-related 
agencies represented at SHARE - for example.

My guess, however, is that you are correct that when you talk to CONTRACTORS (as 
you say above) they may well not see this.  Almost all of those that I have 
dealt with indicate that most of the programmers in their shops are government 
employees and NOT contractors.

Besides IBM, it is my understanding that this is also still a major profit 
center for Unisys - but this is  more from 3rd handy reports than direct 
contacts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T23:41:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a40eg3lhpq42nc6na7rf64qgenfsfvt95b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <5mhh7dFdjddnU1@mid.individual.net> <p0c8g3986h32mqpsbeihodbo89oasek6um@4ax.com> <5mjv1eFdkjtjU3@mid.individual.net> <9rgbg3hpt85mojn8lv6vnnp4okdnli5c4k@4ax.com> <86kcg3dd65dkgdfe4o17srmecnee3emi5t@4ax.com> <dptcg3djgstirp8e7cv1gpka0ca3ub0tq0@4ax.com> <7IwNi.37752$vo5.23616@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Oct 2007 19:59:00 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:dptcg3djgstirp8e7cv1gpka0ca3ub0tq0@4ax.com...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>agencies represented at SHARE - for example.

I read that during the 1990s, the number of *new* IBM mainframe shops was running at 40
per year. After IBM offered Linux in 2000, the number tripled to 120. 

Without statistics on upgrades, redeployments and new development, I assume they're close
to the same proportion -- two-thirds Linux rather than z/OS. IBM's growth comes mostly
from using the mainframe as a big server. Considering that an IFL, a *single*  CPU
dedicated to running Linux, has a price tag of $95K, I don't see how it can be cost
justified. Other single CPU servers just as fast sell for less than $10K. 

http://www-03.ibm.com/systems/z/os/linux/ifl.html

My point is that mainframe growth doesn't mean z/OS growth. I'd love to see statistics on
z/OS use over the last 15 years. If the measure is MIPS, it would have to be discounted by
Moore's Law (24 month).

You said Linux runs under z/VM. On an IFL, it can run on bare metal without VM.

>My guess, however, is that you are correct that when you talk to CONTRACTORS (as 
>you say above) they may well not see this.  Almost all of those that I have 
>dealt with indicate that most of the programmers in their shops are government 
>employees and NOT contractors.

The Feds don't hire many individual contractors, but they outsource many large projects.
Those workers aren't "in their [government] shops", they work in their own shops. 

One GAO report shows the Federal government spends $500M with IBM and $550M with Unisys.
Of course, some of that is for p- and i-series. Neither Sun nor HP appear on the list;
those machines must be under a broker. Dell sells $500M. Computer Sciences (CSC), which is
mostly IT outsourcing, collected $1.6B.. more than IBM and Unisys combined. The IT Group
(now Shaw Group) got $671M. Oracle got $212M.

www.gao.gov/cgi-bin/getrpt?GAO-04-293
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Linux on IBM mainframes was Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-10-07T22:40:03-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k92jg3l334rfhs4q95s4uksj1vmf2rbve1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <5mhh7dFdjddnU1@mid.individual.net> <p0c8g3986h32mqpsbeihodbo89oasek6um@4ax.com> <5mjv1eFdkjtjU3@mid.individual.net> <9rgbg3hpt85mojn8lv6vnnp4okdnli5c4k@4ax.com> <86kcg3dd65dkgdfe4o17srmecnee3emi5t@4ax.com> <dptcg3djgstirp8e7cv1gpka0ca3ub0tq0@4ax.com> <7IwNi.37752$vo5.23616@fe04.news.easynews.com> <a40eg3lhpq42nc6na7rf64qgenfsfvt95b@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Oct 2007 23:41:43 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>> much snipped
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>justified. Other single CPU servers just as fast sell for less than $10K. 

If you use VM, you can run multiple Linux servers in the IFL.  For
non-CPU bound type servers, this can be an extremely cost effective
solution.  Note that the mainframe architecture offloads at least some
of the CPU type work to the controllers and it is the entire package
of things that needs to be evaluated.  I wouldn't try to run Google
servers on a mainframe without a lot of investigation and I would
still be very skeptical that it would work.

>> rest snipped

Clark Morris
```

```
On 4 Oct, 01:55, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On 3 Oct 2007 12:45:34 GMT, b...@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon) wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming standards and lousy code, run scripts that look like JCL.

When I last worked at a  Unix shop I had to write scripts which did
validation prior to executing the batch program. I was shocked to
discover (the hard way) that Unix scripts happily process crap without
falling over and without producing error messages. Has the Unix world
moved on since 2000 or would I still have to check every Unix script
command for correct execution upon completion of that command?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Unix scripting

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T18:58:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uftag319i7mumnt8sbq525t95ih96322g5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <5mhh7dFdjddnU1@mid.individual.net> <p0c8g3986h32mqpsbeihodbo89oasek6um@4ax.com> <1191532668.477797.95140@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 04 Oct 2007 14:17:48 -0700, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 4 Oct, 01:55, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On 3 Oct 2007 12:45:34 GMT, b...@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>command for correct execution upon completion of that command?



Every command exits with a return code, just like every program does. If success is
important, you need to check the return code. Not every command is important. 

If you're lazy, 'set -e' makes the script terminate on any unhandled error. It was
available long before 2000. You can check the syntax of a script with 'set -n'. You can
debug a script i.e. print every line after expansion with 'set -x' . You can also write an
out of line error handler with the trap command.

A polished way to handle errors is with conditional execution, as illustrated here:

      cobol myprog
&& link myprog
&& deploy myprog
&& echo It worked!
||   echo Your build failed. Correct errors and try again.

mkdir new_directory
|| echo Could not make new_directory; exit 16
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T12:23:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 3 Oct 2007 15:19:34 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> growing enough to be significant?

Not sure if you are asking me or Bill, Howard.

In case it's me, here's what I think: :-)

1. Very little OO COBOL is being used.

2. No, the growth is not significant enough to impact the demise of COBOL.

This, to me, is a tragedy.  Vendors (Fujitsu and MicroFocus, and I believe 
there are some others...even IBM) went to great trouble to include OO 
support into their products. (Why do you suppose they did that?) It was an 
amazing feat of software engineering. Nobody cared. Smugness, arrogance, 
inertia, and ignorance on the part of the user community decided it was not 
needed.

Now, there are some in the COBOL community who are starting to realise that 
maybe there could be something in this alternative paradigm. Too little too 
late.

It isn't just about OO, it is about different ways of developing systems. 
Component based systems,  built around encapsulated objects, require less 
maintenance, less regression testing, are more flexible, get developed 
faster using different development methodologies that are iterative and 
interactive (evolutionary), and get many times more reuse than thousands of 
lines of procedural code. It's like building a house with prefabricated 
steel, glass and concrete, as opposed to using bricks...

But apart from that, there is growing disillusionment in industry with the 
spiralling cost of procedural code. It is labour intensive and  many (I 
personally know of five, and I have been out of circulation for a few years 
now; I will be interested to see if this trend is reflected in Europe next 
year) companies are finding that the costs of in-house development on the 
traditional mainframe cannot be warranted. Using Java shows major savings 
over using COBOL, but, my personal opinion is that many companies will move 
off Java too in the (near?) future and decide their core processing can be 
done by a package, and they will retain minimal staff to simply keep the 
network running and provide a helpdesk for departments who will use their 
own spreadsheets and databases, built with standard tools. These "local 
nodes" will be networked, and required to share critical data with the core 
package. The need for IT "specialists" will gradually be removed or 
outsourced. Software will get smarter and the end users will be more 
computer literate. The rising generation don't know what it is to NOT have a 
computer and the Internet is as familiar to them as their cell phones and 
texting.

Eventually, web services will inherit the Earth and any function you need 
will be available as a service from a server somewhere. The software 
industry will go on for the foreseeable future, but in-house development 
won't.

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ml9fiFdo1a3U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <sos9g35dn2dnvtq8u23b66tses9k0inls7@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:sos9g35dn2dnvtq8u23b66tses9k0inls7@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 4 Oct 2007 12:23:58 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> other languages instead of CoBOL for their OO systems?

As always, that is a very incisive observation :-)

I think I feel sad about it  for the following reasons (and you have caused 
me to re-evaluate this...)

1. The wasted effort in providing a facility that SHOULD have been lapped 
up, but wasn't. (I still think the inclusion of OO in COBOL was a work of 
art, despite my "Jake the Peg" comments.)
2. The missed opportunity to keep COBOL in the list of active languages, and 
that would have enabled COBOL programmers to make an easy transition to the 
new paradigm. (Well, in theory it should have. I had great difficulty in 
teaching myself OO COBOL when it first arrived, as I've mentioned here 
before. Two weeks of Java solved this.)
3. The many COBOL programmers who no longer have a job, because of 
stupidity, inertia, and ignorance by themselves and their management.
4. It is hard to make a living out of something for decades, then see it go 
down the gurgler for no good reason, even though I try to be unemotional 
about computer programming languages. What makes me sad in COBOL's case is 
that the vendors did everything necessary to keep COBOL up with the "State 
of the Art", but the user community, for the most part, rejected it. I LIKE 
COBOL. I'm sorry it is no longer relevant for anything I would want to do... 
(network programming and component based systems)

Actually, I'm OK with it now. :-)

Guess I'm taking comfort in new frontiers in C#... :-).

>
> (CoBOL programmers lost, but there are always losers when change
> happens)

That is generally true... and it wasn't like there were no warnings. I've 
been telling people here to expand their skill sets for close on 15 years 
now.
>
> ...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> And so it goes.

Yep. I won't argue with that :-)

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I6qNi.130$Qa.19@bignews4.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <sos9g35dn2dnvtq8u23b66tses9k0inls7@4ax.com> <5ml9fiFdo1a3U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> 1. The wasted effort in providing a facility that SHOULD have been lapped up, but wasn't. (I still think the inclusion of OO in 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> sorry it is no longer relevant for anything I would want to do... (network programming and component based systems)

5. We've thrown away longevity in software. In 1988-89 I wrote a PC based
client/server inventory system for a local company that warehouses and ships
building materials for other companies. The system was originally written for
DOS clients with a Novell server and LAN, Btrieve database, about 100k LOC
COBOL. The system is now a 32bit application running on WinXP clients and
Win 2003 servers with TCP/IP LAN, Btrieve, and is over 130k LOC. The code
changes needed to go from DOS to Win32 were negligible, almost entirely
done in the compiler setup, not the source.

At the time I proposed the system to the client, I suggested using COBOL in
part because of the longevity of the systems. If I were proposing such a system
today, I wouldn't even mention longevity, because I don't see any platform that
I would expect to be running without significant rewrite in 20 years. I don't
know about you, but this makes me sad.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T14:43:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
>>> On 10/3/2007 at 5:23 PM, in message
<5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> 
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>You may have missed a paradigm shift, Bill. The world moved to OO and
the
>>>web. Even though COBOL CAN be used for these thngs (I have been writing 
> OO
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> needed.

One reason that you don't mention here is the actual difficulty that many
Cobol programmers have going from the 'procedural programming paradigm' to
the 'object oriented programming paradigm'.

I believe that you yourself commented that you had a hard time learning OO
Cobol until you learned Java first.  I believe that there are two possible
reasons for this:
1) OO Cobol is poorly designed for OO programming.
2) There is a lack of good resources that teach a Cobol programmer OO
design.

While I know that there are some OO Cobol books out there, I have yet to
find one that is good at teaching a Cobol programmer with no background in
OO design how to actually use OO in a manner that is both consistent with
good OO design and with good Cobol design.

Is OO Cobol perfect?  No.  But I think for the most part OO Cobol is fairly
consistent with how OO works in other OO languages.  In this, it seems to me
that it's possibly easier for a Java programmer to learn OO Cobol than it is
for a Cobol programmer to learn OO Cobol!  Just a guess, of course, with
nothing to back it up.

I would love to see a book that takes a *non-trivial* Cobol application and
refactors it in to "OO Cobol".  Of course books rarely deal with non-trivial
applications, so perhaps this is too much to ask for...

> Now, there are some in the COBOL community who are starting to realise 
> that 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> year) companies are finding that the costs of in-house development on the

> 
> traditional mainframe cannot be warranted. Using Java shows major 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't.

I agree with much of this (though I don't know if I'll ever agree with your
'no source code maintenance' thoughts <g>).
I don't see that the need of IT specialists will be eliminated or even
outsourced, however.  After all, someone still has to develop those
web-services.  This someone, in my opinion, will be an "IT specialist" who
is also familiar with the business, in order to know how best to design the
service.  Having a business user design a spec for implementation by someone
who does not know the business seems to me to be a recipe for disaster!

Frank
```

- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```


"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com...
>>>> On 10/3/2007 at 5:23 PM, in message
> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net>,
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
> the 'object oriented programming paradigm'.

Geez, Frank, I've covered it at length in this forum, on numerous occasions; 
not much point repeating it again. Having had to train some COBOLlers into 
OO I realise how difficult it is, even leaving aside my own experience.
>
> I believe that you yourself commented that you had a hard time learning OO
> Cobol until you learned Java first.
 Thank you :-).


 I believe that there are two possible
> reasons for this:
> 1) OO Cobol is poorly designed for OO programming.

I disagree with that one. They did a great job of adding OO support. There 
is very little I can do in Java  (a pure OO language from day 1) that I 
can't do in OO COBOL, and OO COBOL even supports multiple inheritance, which 
Java does not. The "problem" for OO COBOL is that it has inherited COBOL's 
verbosity; a remnant of an older time when source code was king and "self 
documentation" was a major selling point.  Who's going to write:

invoke myObject "Dothebiz"
           using  parm1, parm2, parm3
           returning BoolResult
end-invoke

...when they can write:

Boolean BoolResult = myObject.Dothebiz (parm1, parm2, parm3);

Functionally identical, but the second one has simplicity and elegance (not 
to mention being much quicker to code, especially if you have Intellisense).

> 2) There is a lack of good resources that teach a Cobol programmer OO
> design.

The language should not be a factor when learning design.

>
> While I know that there are some OO Cobol books out there, I have yet to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> nothing to back it up.

I can back it up from my own experience. :-)

>
> I would love to see a book that takes a *non-trivial* Cobol application 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, don't look at me... :-)

Even if I was tempted to write such a book, I wouldn't. Why not? Because it 
is irrelevant unless there is a user community who want it, and they have 
already shown that they don't. Therefore ther's nothing in it for the 
author, except a labour of love and a huge amount of time and effort that, 
in the long run is pointless.

>> Now, there are some in the COBOL community who are starting to realise
>> that
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'no source code maintenance' thoughts <g>).

If you use OO extensively, and, particularly if you are building component 
based systems... which is ALL I have been building for the last 10 years or 
so...), you will eventually see what I am talking about. :-)

> I don't see that the need of IT specialists will be eliminated or even
> outsourced, however.  After all, someone still has to develop those
> web-services.

Frank, do you have any idea how EASY it is to build a web service, even 
today?

I can go into VS 2005 and tell it I want a Web Service. Whatever code I 
write will AUTOMATICALLY have the requisite SOAP and WSDL layers generated 
instantly, as soon as I build the project. I get an .ASMX page ready for 
immediate deployment to my web server.

The first web service I built 
(http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/AVSWebService.asmx ) actually 
wraps COBOL code and runs it from C#.

Click the "service description" and it reveals all the WSDL. I didn't write 
a single line of it :-)

Click the "ValidateNZaddress" method and it shows a description of the 
method, and sample SOAP you could use to access it. I didn't write a line of 
it :-)

It was all just mouse clicks in VS 2005.

I note from the server log that there have been some attempts to run the 
service. Obviously, I have protected it, and these attempts all failed. 
(although the service is published, implying it is available for use, I 
don't want it accessed except by the channels I authorize)

(This is a pre-release web site that I haven't completed yet. I hope to get 
it finished in the next few weeks. Paid work obviously gets priority :-))

If you actually want to try the service 
(http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebSite/DemoInteractive.aspx) you can see 
the current interactive demo. Now, this I DID write :-)

(It is C# and ASP.NET code that drives it, but the actual work is done by an 
engine written as a component in OO COBOL (around 4000 lines). It is all 
running on my web server in San Francisco, yet the response times are quite 
surprising. If you input 3 addresses (there are sample ones on the page you 
can cut and paste, or, if you know anybody in NZ try inputting their address 
and get the new post code for it) you can see the response improve as 
buffers are loaded and objects instantiated)

My point is that tools are becoming so powerful that it won't be long before 
end users (who are also becoming more computer savvy) will build whatever 
they need for themselves.

> This someone, in my opinion, will be an "IT specialist" who
> is also familiar with the business, in order to know how best to design 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Absolutely! We KNOW that doesn't work... :-)

Why have a spec. at all? That's the whole point about JAD, Use Cases, object 
modelling, and iterative, interactive development.The Use Case defines the 
requirement; if code is seen to work in every path of the Use Case then 
that's it. (Use Cases and Use Case paths can share components, and this cuts 
development time dramatically, but you need object modelling to define the 
components in the first place...). The accepted running code reflects the 
required functionality.

The "spec." can be written after the code is delivered, if you really want 
to waste time... :-)) In fact, languages like C# can generate the 
documentation for you. (It writes XML so it can be viewed anywhere...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and COBOL (was: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-06T00:51:49+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mmmqmFad3kqU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net> <bjjNi.34745$vo5.2462@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:bjjNi.34745$vo5.2462@fe04.news.easynews.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>  How much more verbose is that???

Sure. But not all OO COBOL implementations support the shorthand notation 
and to a COBOL eye it is ugly. (Neither Jimmy Gavan nor myself opted to use 
it when we first started playing with OO COBOL. Now that the scales have 
fallen from mine eyes, I don't find it so offensive :-))

The other thing you're missing is Intellisense; I wouldn't be typing more 
than the first couple of characters of all these names, whereas in the COBOL 
IDEs I have to type the lot.

Furthermore COBOL doesn't have language standard values for TRUE and FALSE 
or a Boolean type. I had major problems trying to make VB talk to Power 
COBOL because of that one particular point. (And there's no support... 
there's no one you can ask and get an immediate response, so you spend hours 
poring over manuals and searching for clues... It is bloody painful and it 
is only now that I don't have to do it anymore that I realise how much time 
I spent banging my head against a wall trying to get things done in COBOL 
that it just wasn't built for. I spent a day just trying to get a standard 
file selection dialog working properly from COBOL. :-) (I can laugh about it 
now...))

Nevertheless, it is a good response Bill and, as a single example, a fair 
one.

I'll move back to OO COBOL when it has :

1. Reflection. (of Language Metadata)
2. Delegate classes.
3. Try...catch... finally... blocks (for EVERYTHING and ANYWHERE you want 
them).
4. Seamless integration with the environment (no API calls or CBL 
subroutines required), and Scripting.
5. Collections that can be enumerated with Foreach.(I LOVE this concept... 
:-))
6. Debugging that can break on ANY statement (even a brace in a block)
7. Regular Expressions.
8. Uses the DotNET framework as a Class Library.
9. Supports Query Expressions and Lamdas.
10. Can give me immediate support from a community of millions over 
Broadband Internet, for free.
11. Lets me download sample code instantly if I'm not sure how to do 
something.
12. Support for Web Development and Web Services.
13. Support for Network development and RPC, easily and simply.
14. Support for cell phones and hand held devices.
15. IntelliSense in the IDE.
16. No runtime licensing.
17. The compiler costs less than $50, or is free.
18. A cutdown version of the IDE, including IntelliSense, is free.

OR

Satan wears an anorak....:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and COBOL (was: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net> <bjjNi.34745$vo5.2462@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mmmqmFad3kqU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5mmmqmFad3kqU1@mid.individual.net...
[snip]
> Furthermore COBOL doesn't have language standard values for TRUE and FALSE
> or a Boolean type.

For COBOL 2002,
    USAGE BIT PIC 1
defines a 1-bit boolean. A value of 1 is TRUE
and 0 is FALSE.

> I had major problems trying to make VB talk to Power
> COBOL because of that one particular point.

VB 5.0 used a 16-bit integer for Boolean, where 0 was
FALSE and not 0 (normally -1) was TRUE.
```

```
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message

The 2002 standard addresses a number of things that would improve COBOL. 
Unfortunately, it is unreadable and (probably) unimplementable.

>
>> I had major problems trying to make VB talk to Power
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, I found that out :-) It was painful and it shouldn't have been. Power 
COBOL also used a 16 bit integer  "for compatibility" with VB but it had to 
be set wth POW-TRUE and POW-FALSE rather than TRUE and FALSE.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T09:45:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qokcg3d8bpnf2tkno4cc7df5ekkdmbjedo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 5 Oct 2007 13:08:17 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Java does not. The "problem" for OO COBOL is that it has inherited COBOL's 
>verbosity; a remnant of an older time when source code was king and "self 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Boolean BoolResult = myObject.Dothebiz (parm1, parm2, parm3);

CoBOL has tried to keep its "English language" roots - but when it has
moved into concepts that aren't in English, it has instead added
confusion.

The CALL command uses the English words "Reference", "Value", &
"Content", as though that makes those concepts understandable.   When
someone asks what they mean, we usually give them examples in other
computer languages.
```

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **Date:** 2007-10-06T19:10:39-06:00

- **References:** `<a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net> <t2ncg3poph074m5neq3uju3s5hjj43vbuk@4ax.com> <5mnt0uFechjkU1@mid.individual.net> <uNKdnaWdwOrca5ranZ2dnUVZ_rignZ2d@comcast.com> <5mqp2rFes580U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:uNKdnaWdwOrca5ranZ2dnUVZ_rignZ2d@comcast.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> missing something here? :-)

You don't have to catch or declare exceptions in C# - by default, the 
program will run, and they'll bubble up to the top and come out as 
"Server Error in '/' Application" or some such thing.  :)  In Java, the 
code won't build unless you catch the exception or declare that your 
method throws it.  When I first started programming in Java, I hated 
that - now, I find it very useful.  Everything is out in the open and 
must be dealt with - kind of like an agile scrum team...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-07T08:53:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ey5Oi.1254$lE2.294@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net> <t2ncg3poph074m5neq3uju3s5hjj43vbuk@4ax.com> <5mnt0uFechjkU1@mid.individual.net> <uNKdnaWdwOrca5ranZ2dnUVZ_rignZ2d@comcast.com> <5mqp2rFes580U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Hate to be so late in on this one, but...

The question, "How proprietary is the 'COBOL file system?' "  is a trick 
question.

COBOL does not define a file system.

And unless I am mistaken, even for data as elementary as PIC X(10) in a file 
defined with ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL (aka "a plain 
flat file")  COBOL itself says absolutely nothing about PHYSICAL storage in 
*any* particular medium.  It's only after a successful READ -  when the data 
are in LOGICAL storage - that bit patterns and lengths are defined (except 
for USAGEs defined as 'implementor -defined').

I suspect the actual question should have been  "How portable are 
COBOL-created data across both operating systems and/or different COBOL 
implementations ("brand name compilers")?"



MCM
```

```
news:ey5Oi.1254$lE2.294@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net...
> Hate to be so late in on this one, but...

> question.

No, Michael, nothing tricky about it. The IBM documentation (and Fujitsu... 
possibly others...) refer to the "COBOL file system".

I realised there would be objections on the grounds you have stated and 
that's why I put it in quotes.
>
> COBOL does not define a file system.

certainly in at least two implementations I have worked with.

>
> And unless I am mistaken, even for data as elementary as PIC X(10) in a 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> defined (except for USAGEs defined as 'implementor -defined').

Yes. That's correct.

My question was only about INDEXED organisations.
>
> I suspect the actual question should have been  "How portable are 
> COBOL-created data across both operating systems and/or different COBOL 
> implementations ("brand name compilers")?"

I'm pretty happy with what I wrote and the responses received (including

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-07T15:42:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<687Oi.66770$6L.26682@fe03.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <4704FC0F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5mldjjFe4t8eU1@mid.individual.net> <t2ncg3poph074m5neq3uju3s5hjj43vbuk@4ax.com> <5mnt0uFechjkU1@mid.individual.net> <uNKdnaWdwOrca5ranZ2dnUVZ_rignZ2d@comcast.com> <5mqp2rFes580U1@mid.individual.net> <ey5Oi.1254$lE2.294@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net> <5ms7fiFf201bU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete,
  When you say that IBM uses the term "COBOL file system" is this an historical 
statment, a Windows COBOL documentation statement, or something else.

I have QUICKLY checked the books at:
   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/Shelves/IGY3SH33

and I couldn't find this phrase used.  However, their online search rules are 
bad enough that it might be there.  If you have some references, I would be 
interested in seeing them.  In general (on the mainframe at least), it would 
surprise me to see this term used, as the "file system" in these IBM 
environments "belongs" to the O/S and not to COBOL.  See for example,
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr31/4.2.1
where they talk about "access methods" which in this environment is the (O/S) 
file system(s).

I did find references to "COBOL file system" in the Fujitsu (NetCOBOL for 
Windows documetnation, for example in
   "Table 7.8 Functional differences between file systems"

but in that table it seems to be used to distinguish the Fujitsu provided file 
system from "others" where it has one column called "COBOL file system" next to 
other columns called "Btriev" and " RDM"
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:687Oi.66770$6L.26682@fe03.news.easynews.com...
> Pete,
>  When you say that IBM uses the term "COBOL file system" is this an 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> (O/S) file system(s).

Fair enough. They have obviously moved on...

My reference was "historical" and referred to DOS/VS and OS/VS COBOL where 
there were chapters on the "COBOL file system".

At least, that's how I remember it. :-)

Pretty sure that the manual for the NCR 315 had a chapter on it too.

Is my point weakened if I'm wrong about this? My post was about 
interoperability of INDEXED file systems. That's all.

It just occurred to me, after several decades of using them and always 
considering they were "portable" (because programmers think at a source code 
level), that they are no more portable than a class library.

Pete.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 5 Oct 2007 13:08:17 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> think I disagree with that choice.

I'm with you.  I had a run-in with that just last week.  I'm using some 
code from the Navy, and the service all extend another class.  However, 
all of our services extend one of our classes, and our base class does 
things like handle database access, etc.  I was banging my head on the 
desk (figuratively, of course) trying to figure this one out.

I finally asked one of the folks that have been there a long time, and 
he suggested making an abstract class that extends the Navy one, 
implements the database and user session stuff I needed, then extending 
*it* for my detail services.  Works like a charm, and makes more sense 
to me than extending both.
```

```
>>> is irrelevant unless there is a user community who want it, and they
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> For you, Frank, I'll write such a book... :-) Unfortunately, the cost may

> be 
> a bit more than you would be prepared to pay...

Pay?  Who said anything about paying for it?  :-)
 
> Seriously, it probably wouldn't hurt to explore some of this in an

Frank
```

```
On Thu, 4 Oct 2007 12:23:58 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>1. Very little OO COBOL is being used.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>support into their products. 

Don't say even IBM. I'm pretty sure Ann Bennett of IBM was the leader who pushed OO Cobol
through the standards process. 

>(Why do you suppose they did that?) It was an 
>amazing feat of software engineering. Nobody cared. Smugness, arrogance, 
>inertia, and ignorance on the part of the user community decided it was not 
>needed.

Did you really expect people clinging to PERFORM THRU and numbered paragraphs to switch to
OO Cobol?

>It isn't just about OO, it is about different ways of developing systems. 
>Component based systems,  built around encapsulated objects, require less 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>steel, glass and concrete, as opposed to using bricks...

We've always had reusable code. We just copy and paste from old programs to new ones. 

If we change a component, we have to re-test every system that uses it.

>But apart from that, there is growing disillusionment in industry with the 
>spiralling cost of procedural code. It is labour intensive and  many (I 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>texting.

They're all familiar with viruses, pfishing, spam and identity theft. They think bad guys
are smarter than administrators and are vary nervous about public networks. 

2005 Cardsystems reveals 40 million credit card numbers with name, expiration date and
security code.

2005 Mizuho, largest bank in Japan, loses 270,000 bank accounts, says they were probably
not stolen.

2005 Bulldog Broadband (UK) reveals 100,000 customer records, with credit card and bank
account numbers.

2007 TJ Maxx learns hackers have been in its system four years. Potential exposure 40
million accounts. 

2007 Russian jackers steal 1.3 million account details from Monster.com job site.

>Eventually, web services will inherit the Earth and any function you need 
>will be available as a service from a server somewhere. The software 
>industry will go on for the foreseeable future, but in-house development 
>won't.

Except companies paranoid about security. The 10% who aren't will use web services.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How proprietary is the "COBOL file system"

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T17:34:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mlt6iFe8arvU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5m8mc4Fcb1ukU1@mid.individual.net> <a8huf3ppubdf9pd0gmtuiiv3s5efn19r0b@4ax.com> <5m953cFc85a2U1@mid.individual.net> <9H8Mi.8185$or1.184@newsfe19.lga> <5mdff6Fcooh8U1@mid.individual.net> <5mesn3Fd7fo5U1@mid.individual.net> <47m4g3tm2lirakmdvg0t1j8i6ruls60qa0@4ax.com> <5mfdb5Fdbh7iU1@mid.individual.net> <5mgchvFd9eqeU1@mid.individual.net> <bsc7g316u2gfrjltfjr24jodq99hcjckbe@4ax.com> <5mimkgFdq8tqU1@mid.individual.net> <qn7bg3l825lo3co70bmosrf9pdcutq07gj@4ax.com>`
