##### ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T11:09:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TFpMi.37948$RX.23786@newssvr11.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <13g3q5roin9v14b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13g3q5roin9v14b@corp.supernews.com>, Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.nospam.com.invalid> wrote:
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> Here is some current production code:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>               ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - RSRV-ACC-INT - .005.

Well, there are two problem with changing the plus to a minus:

First, the original implementation was wrong, so preserving its effect doesn't 
achieve the desired result.

And second, rounding can be accomplished by adding 1/2 of the least 
significant digit (0.005 in this case) and *truncating*, or by using the 
ROUNDED phrase alone, but it is an error to add 1/2 *and* use the ROUNDED 
phrase. This will produce incorrect results 50% of the time.
```

#### ↳ Re: rounding

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T14:06:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
I'm going to start over a bit and describe the issue in more detail.  I will
include the reason why I am doing what appears to be 'pre-mature' rounding. 
You can decide whether or not this is justified...

Here are the relevant fields:

reserve limit (RSRV-LIMIT) PIC S9(7) COMP-3 (whole dollars only)
reserve balance owing (RSRV-BALANCE) S9(7)V99 COMP-3
reserve interest accrued (RSRV-ACC-INT) S9(6)999 COMP-3 (note three decimal
positions)

There is also reserve available, but it is a calculated field.  In general,
here is how it is calculated:

compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance

So if you have a reserve limit of $4500 and you've used $1000 you have $3500
available to use.

This is pretty obvious.  Here's when it gets a bit weird...
Let's say that you own $1000 (principal) plus 5.097 accrued interest.  If
you wanted to pay down the entire amount, including the interest, your
payment would be 1005.10, because we rounded the interest up.  (Don't worry,
if the interest was 5.094 you'd pay only the 5.09 in interest!  <g>)

Anyway, lets say you call in and say you want to pay $500 of the reserve
from your savings account.  Once you did this we'd immediately bump up your
available reserve by that $500.  So now our calculating looks like this:

compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance + pending-payments

Thus: 4500 - 1000 + 500 = 4000 available

Again, all very simple and makes sense.
But what if, you want to may off the entire balance plus the interest.  So,
in addition to the $500 you did a minute ago you do another payment of
$505.10).  So now there's a total payment of $1005.10.  We may 1000 to the
reserve and 5.10 to the accrued interest.

One thing to explain.  All of these are simply 'memo posts', as it were.  We
don't post real time, so technically the balance is still $1000 and the
accrued interest is still $5.097.  But we do adjust your available to use
based on the payments that are pending to post.

Anyway, if we were to use the above calculation we'd end up having
rsrv-avail as $4505.10.  But this is not right, because the extra $5.10 is
being paid to the bank and should not affect your available to use amount. 
So here's part of how the calculation works now...

if pending-payments > rsrv-balance
    move rsrv-balance to pending-payments
end-if
compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance + pending-payments

All we're doing here is say that if your payments exceed the principal owed,
when making the calculation we'll only count the payments that don't exceed
the principal owed.

Slightly confusing, but still fairly straight forward.  Let's give some
examples:

You owe $1000 + $5.10

If you pay $1000, then we add the $1000 back when calculating your
available, giving you the entire $4500.
If you pay $1005.10 then we add back only the $1000 from the principal
(rsrv-balance), again giving you $4500 available.
If you pay $1003.00 then we again add back only the $1000 from the
principal, again giving you $4500.
But what if, for whatever weird reason, you make a payment of $1010?

First I'll describe what would happen during posting, forgetting for the
moment 'available reserve'.

- We would credit $1000 to the balance owing, leaving you with a total
balance owing of $0.
- We would credit $5.10 toward the accrued interest, leaving no interest
owing
- We would credit the remainder ($4.90) back to your checking account,
increasing it's balance by this amount

In the 'real time' world what we end up doing (or trying to do!) is giving
you an available reserve of $4504.90, which is your credit limit plus the
amount over-paid.

Thus, we end up with the probably not quite right code:


if pending-payments > rsrv-balance
    if pending-payments > rsrv-balance + rsrv-acc-int
        compute pending-payments rounded =
                pending-payments - (rsrv-acc-int +.005)
    else
        move rsrv-balance to pending-payments
    end-if
end-if
compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance + pending-payments

Our goal here is to have 'pending payments' in this case be 1004.90, so that
rsrv-avail ends up being 4504.90.
Specifically, $4500 - $1000 + $1004.90.  
(I guess we should call it 'pending-principal-payments-and-overpayments',
but gosh is that verbose!).

Anyway, let's see if the code actually calculates this correctly:

pending-payments starts out as $1010.
$1010 is greater than rsrv-balance of $1000
$1010 is greater than rsrv-balance + rsrv-acc-int ($1005.097)
So we use the 'compute pending-payments' statement above...
1010.00 - (5.097 + 0.005)
1010.00 - (5.102)
1004.898
rounded to 1004.90

And now we use this in our final compute
4500.00 - 1000.00 + 1004.90 = 4504.90
This is the result I wanted.

But!  Let's say that the accrued interest is due to be rounded down; for
example 5.094.  Now our first compute looks like this:
1010.00 - (5.094 + 0.005)
1010.00 - (5.099)
1004.901
rounded to 1004.90

This is not the desired result, however.  The desired result is,
essentially
pending-payments - accrued-interest-to-pay-if-all-paid-today

accrued-interest-to-pay-if-all-paid-today is simple actual accrued interest
rounded from three decimal positions to two decimal positions.
Thus, in this case 5.09, and not 5.10.

All this being said, this is why I chose to round first, before subtracting,
eg:

if pending-payments > rsrv-balance
    if pending-payments > rsrv-balance + rsrv-acc-int
        compute rsrv-acc-int-rounded rounded = rsrv-acc-int
        compute pending-payments =
                pending-payments - rsrv-acc-int-rounded
    else
        move rsrv-balance to pending-payments
    end-if
end-if
compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance + pending-payments

Thus, we have:
1010 - 5.09
1004.91
Which, gives us a rsrv-avail of
4500.00 - 1000 + 1004.91
= 4504.91

In other words, the over-payment now is a penny more than before because
with the original accrued interest (5.097) we 'paid' 5.10, whereas with the
new accrued interest (5.094) we 'paid' only 5.09.

All this being said, I also did the following and got the same results as
with the above:

if pending-payments > rsrv-balance
    if pending-payments > rsrv-balance + rsrv-acc-int
        subtract rsrv-acc-int from pending-payments rounded
    else
        move rsrv-balance to pending-payments
    end-if
end-if
compute rsrv-avail = rsrv-limit - rsrv-balance + pending-payments

Let's see how this works...
1010.00 - 5.097
1004.903
round to 1004.90

1010.00 - 5.094
1004.906
round to 1004.91

These are both also the expected results.
But it still makes me nervous because I can't *see* it in my mind always
working.  Nor can I think of an example to disprove it.
I'm thinking that it *should* be right, and it eliminates what appears to be
the 'pre-mature rounding'.  I guess I just need some way of generating
different inputs and seeing if I always get the same results with both
methods.  But certainly I've already proved that the original method is
incorrect.

Thoughts?

Frank
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-10-02T20:36:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
In article <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[major snip]
>But it still makes me nervous because I can't *see* it in my mind always
>working.  Nor can I think of an example to disprove it.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thoughts?

First thought is that the set of test cases needed to perform an exhaustive 
test is small enough that you could have done exhaustive testing, by hand, in 
less time than it took you to type this post.

Second thought is that, no matter how you ultimately go about it, remember 
that there are two correct ways to round:
1) add 1/2 to the last significant digit, and truncate
2) use the ROUNDED phrase
Don't mix them. Adding 1/2 *and* using ROUNDED is *always* wrong: it's 
guaranteed to produce incorrect results 50% of the time, on average.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T09:32:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
>>> On 10/2/2007 at 4:58 PM, in message
<470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
>>>> On 10/2/2007 at 2:36 PM, in message
> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com>, Doug Miller<spambait@milmac.com>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>I'm thinking that it *should* be right, and it eliminates what appears
to
> 
>> be
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> in a meeting for the last two hours!!)

The following is five different ways I considered to perform this
calculation.  Method 1 is the 'baseline' and is compared with all other
methods.  None of the others get the exact same results as method 1 100% of
the time.  So unless someone can give me another alternative I'm going to go
with method 1.

Thanks for all of the comments!

       ID DIVISION.                                                     
       PROGRAM-ID. ACCINT.                                                  
   
                                                                            
   
       DATA DIVISION.                                                       
   
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                             
   
       77  RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS           PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3.                 
   
       77  RSRV-ACC-INT                PIC S9(6)V999 COMP-3.                
   
       77  RSRV-ACC-INT-ROUNDED        PIC S9(6)V99 COMP-3.                 
   
       77  ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS            PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3.                 
   
       77  N                           PIC 9.                               
   
                                                                            
   
       01  CREDITS-TABLE.                                                   
   
           05  CREDITS                 PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3                  
   
                                       OCCURS 5 INDEXED BY CIDX.            
   
                                                                            
   
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                                  
   
           MOVE 100 TO RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS                                    
   
           DISPLAY '***** ' RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS ' *****'                      
   
           PERFORM VARYING RSRV-ACC-INT FROM ZERO BY 0.001                  
   
                     UNTIL RSRV-ACC-INT > 1.000                             
   
               COMPUTE RSRV-ACC-INT-ROUNDED ROUNDED = RSRV-ACC-INT          
   
               PERFORM HANDLE-A                                             
   
               PERFORM HANDLE-B                                             
   
               PERFORM HANDLE-C                                             
   
               PERFORM HANDLE-D                                             
   
               PERFORM HANDLE-E                                             
   
               PERFORM COMPARE-CREDITS                                      
   
           END-PERFORM                                                      
   
           STOP RUN.                                                        
   
                                                                            
   
       HANDLE-A.                                                            
   
           MOVE RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS TO ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS                       
   
           SET CIDX TO 1                                                    
   
           COMPUTE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS =                                       
   
                   ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - RSRV-ACC-INT-ROUNDED                  
   
           PERFORM STORE-IT                                                 
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       HANDLE-B.                                                            
   
           MOVE RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS TO ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS                       
   
           SET CIDX TO 2                                                    
   
           COMPUTE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS ROUNDED =                               
   
                   ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - (RSRV-ACC-INT + .005)                 
   
           PERFORM STORE-IT                                                 
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       HANDLE-C.                                                            
   
           MOVE RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS TO ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS                       
   
           SET CIDX TO 3                                                    
   
           COMPUTE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS ROUNDED =                               
   
                   ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - RSRV-ACC-INT                          
   
           PERFORM STORE-IT                                                 
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       HANDLE-D.                                                            
   
           MOVE RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS TO ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS                       
   
           SET CIDX TO 4                                                    
   
           COMPUTE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS =                                       
   
                   ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - RSRV-ACC-INT + .005                   
   
           PERFORM STORE-IT                                                 
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       HANDLE-E.                                                            
   
           MOVE RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS TO ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS                       
   
           SET CIDX TO 5                                                    
   
           COMPUTE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS =                                       
   
                   ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS - (RSRV-ACC-INT + .005)                 
   
           PERFORM STORE-IT                                                 
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       STORE-IT.                                                            
   
           MOVE ADJ-MEMO-CREDITS TO CREDITS(CIDX)                           
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       COMPARE-CREDITS.                                                     
   
           PERFORM VARYING CIDX FROM 2 BY 1 UNTIL CIDX > 5                  
   
               SET N TO CIDX                                                
   
               IF CREDITS(1) NOT = CREDITS(CIDX)                            
   
                   DISPLAY RSRV-ACC-INT ': (1/' N ') '                      
   
                           CREDITS(1) ' ' CREDITS(CIDX) ' '                 
   
               END-IF                                                       
   
           END-PERFORM                                                      
   
           EXIT.                                                            
   
                                                                            
   
       END PROGRAM ACCINT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T17:55:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
In article <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

>The following is five different ways I considered to perform this
>calculation.  Method 1 is the 'baseline' and is compared with all other
>methods.  

Why are you using as the 'baseline' a method that you've repeatedly been told 
is not correct?

>None of the others get the exact same results as method 1 100% of
>the time.  

That's because method 1 produces wrong answers part of the time, and therefore 
anything that produces right answers all the time won't match it.

>So unless someone can give me another alternative I'm going to go
>with method 1.

NO. Method 1 is WRONG. You're comparing to the wrong thing.

I've pointed out several times now that there are two correct -- and 
equivalent -- methods of rounding: add 1/2 to the least significant digit and 
truncate, or use the ROUNDED phrase. Not both.

It's also been pointed out to you at least twice (once by me, once by Robert) 
that rounding should be done only at the end of the calculation. Keep as many 
significant figures as possible for as long as possible, to ensure the 
greatest possible accuracy of results. 

Methods 3 and 4 are CORRECT, and should match each other 100% of the time. 
Anything that doesn't match these is wrong.

Method 1 is WRONG. Round *after* all calculation is done. *Never* round 
intermediate results. This is guaranteed to introduce errors, as I pointed 
out in my response to your initial post.

Method 2 is WRONG. Round the final result (Method 3), or add 1/2 and truncate 
(Method 4), but (for the third time!) DON'T MIX THEM. And don't subtract and 
truncate.

Method 5 is WRONG. Add 1/2 and truncate (Method 4), but don't subtract and 
truncate.


>
>Thanks for all of the comments!
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>       77  RSRV-MEMO-CREDITS           PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3.                 
  
>       77  RSRV-ACC-INT                PIC S9(6)V999 COMP-3.                
>   
…[156 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T16:35:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4703C4D3.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net>`

```
>>> On 10/3/2007 at 11:55 AM, in message
<mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net>, Doug
Miller<spambait@milmac.com> wrote:
> In article <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>, "Frank Swarbrick" 
> <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> equivalent -- methods of rounding: add 1/2 to the least significant digit

> and 
> truncate, or use the ROUNDED phrase. Not both.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> truncate.

I did not ignore your initial post, or anyone else's.  I explained why
method 1, in fact, is the only one that gives the expected results.  I will
say that I understand the rule that one should round only after all of the
calculations are done.  I believe this to be a special case, though, as the
accrued interest itself will always be rounded in and of itself when a
payment of accrued interest is being made.

Specific examples again:

accrued interest = 0.685
memo credits = 100.00
reserve limit = 1000.00
reserve balance = 50.00

Using method 1:
0.685 rounded = 0.69
100.00 - 0.69 = 99.31

1000.00 - 50.00 + 99.31 = 1049.31

Using method 3:
100.00 - 0.685 = 99.315
rounded = 99.32

1000.00 - 50.00 + 99.32 = 1049.32


The first result (method 1), not the second (method 3), is the correct
result.  This is because, of the payment of $100.00, $50 goes to pay the
balance owing and $0.69 goes to pay the accrued interest.  This leaves
$49.31 as an over payment, which at the end of the night will be move back
to the checking account.  Thus my "available reserve" during the day is
$49.31, not $49.32.


Frank
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T05:44:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P4%Mi.28237$6L.16495@fe03.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net> <4703C4D3.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <TaVMi.108$LD2.92@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net>`

```
"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
news:TaVMi.108$LD2.92@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net...
<snip>
>
> In that case, quite bluntly, your expectations are wrong, too.
>
Doug,
  I think you are mistaken in assuming that a business (any business) can decide 
WHAT logic they want to "run their business".

The example, that comes to mind is US tax forms, where one is allowed to round 
ALL or no intermediate results - but one isn't allowed to round JUST the final 
results (or at least this was the case when I last did my own taxes - several 
years ago).

At a "theoretical" level, this has to be "wrong".  OBVIOUSLY, with rounding of 
all intermediate results (fields) one could have the case where all the item 
"owed to the government" were rounded down (say xx.49) and all the "deductions" 
were rounded up (say xx.51).  In that case, the end results would be WELL over 
$1.00 "off" from what would be calculated with no rounding.

The point is that the "business" decides what rules they want to "follow" and 
the program (in this case) should match THEIR expectations.

I would certainly say that Frank should "run" those cases where the "normal" 
(what you want) cases differ from what he expects by the "management" and make 
certain that the answers he is getting is what they REALLY want.  but if that is 
the case, then that is what the program should do.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net> <4703C4D3.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On Oct 4, 11:35 am, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbr...@efirstbank.com>
wrote:
> >>> On 10/3/2007 at 11:55 AM, in message
>
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> 100.00 - 0.69 = 99.31

The 'accrued interest' is thus _NOT_ an 'intermediate result', it is a
specific result, one that is required to be rounded to the nearest
cent.

Your calculation should be

     COMPUTE accrued_interest ROUNDED = something * rate

Then you can simply subtract this from the credits.

> 1000.00 - 50.00 + 99.31 = 1049.31

     COMPUTE available = reserve_limit - reserve_balance  +
memo_credits -
                               accrued_interest


> Using method 3:
> 100.00 - 0.685 = 99.315
> rounded = 99.32

Exactly, you are rounding at the wrong time. The problem with the
original was it was rounding twice. Once by adding .005, second time
with ROUNDED.

Method 3 is rounding the positive result instead of the negative
interest, thus the rounding goes the wrong way.

> 1000.00 - 50.00 + 99.32 = 1049.32
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Frank
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rounding

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T11:04:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4704C8A8.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<47012C76.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4702507E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <cZxMi.515$oA2.98@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <470278A9.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <4703619B.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <mIQMi.11133$JD.4756@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net> <4703C4D3.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1191469520.884152.110790@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
>>> On 10/3/2007 at 9:45 PM, in message
<1191469520.884152.110790@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>,
Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> On Oct 4, 11:35 am, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbr...@efirstbank.com>
> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >>calculation.  Method 1 is the 'baseline' and is compared with all
other
>> >>methods.
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > equivalent -- methods of rounding: add 1/2 to the least significant
digit
>> > and
>> > truncate, or use the ROUNDED phrase. Not both.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > Method 1 is WRONG. Round *after* all calculation is done. *Never*
round
>> > intermediate results. This is guaranteed to introduce errors, as I
>> > pointed
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>      COMPUTE accrued_interest ROUNDED = something * rate

Well, it is.  Only at this point, it is three decimal positions.  See
below.
 
> Then you can simply subtract this from the credits.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                accrued_interest

I'm not sure if you are agreeing or disagreeing with me, but you have
accurately stated my belief.  In this instance, as you say, accrued interest
is not an intermediate result.  If a payment is made today that pays the
current *rounded* accrued interest then the accrued interest is set to zero
and we start over.  This is done as follows...  (Warning, it's kind of weird
at first glance, but it works.)

The total amount of the payment is subtracted from the reserve balance
owing.  This can, temporarily (a few milliseconds!) make the reserve balance
owing a negative amount.

Reserve Balance Owing = 62.86
Reserve Interest Accrued = 0.153

Payment made for 63.01
Reserve balance owing = -0.15
Reserve Interest Accrued = 0.153

"Loan" credit generated back to account for $0.15
Reserve balance owing = 0.00
Reserve Interest Accrued = 0.153

Finance charge debit to account generated for $0.15

Here's where it gets a bit weird:

    IF D-RSRV-ACC-INT                               
                   GREATER THAN D-SPLT-TRAN-AMOUNT  
        MOVE D-SPLT-TRAN-AMOUNT TO WORKFLD          
        PERFORM 2550-CREATE55-CONTINUE THRU         
                2550-CREATE55-EXIT                  
    ELSE                                            
        COMPUTE WORKFLD ROUNDED EQUAL D-RSRV-ACC-INT
        PERFORM 2550-CREATE55-CONTINUE THRU         
                2550-CREATE55-EXIT.                 

2550-CREATE55-CONTINUE.
    SUBTRACT WORKFLD FROM D-RSRV-ACC-INT.    
    IF D-RSRV-ACC-INT LESS THAN .005         
        MOVE ZEROES TO D-RSRV-ACC-INT.

In this case the accrued interest is more than the transaction amount.  So
we subtract the transaction amount (.15) from the accrued interest (.153)
and get a result of .003.  Since this result is less than .005 we simply
zero out the accrued interest.

[On the other hand, lets say the accrued interest was 1.896.  The payment
for the interest would me 1.90.  This is more than the accrued interest, so
we simply "bump up" the accrued interest to this amount.  We then subtract
this amount (1.90) from what is now the accrued interest (1.90), and now the
accrued interest is zero.  (I'm guessing there might be a more sensible way
to do this and get the same result, but it's been that way since 1989 or so,
so...)]

Now I can see a case could be made where the accrued interest at this point
should be 0.003, rather than zero.  But since there is no way for the
customer to actually pay that 3/10's of a cent, we simply write it off.  Of
course if the accrued interest were an amount requiring rounding up then we
also 'write it off' rather than crediting the customer a partial cent.  If
we were to do this, rather than zeroing accrued interest out when it is less
than .005 and more than -.005, then I believe that the rounding that Doug
and others have been pushing would probably be correct.  But that is simply
not how our business is functioning.

Now there is certainly an option for us to accrue only to two decimal
positions rather than three.  I'm sure there was some business decision made
many years ago to make it the three positions, and round to two positions
only when a full pay-off is made.  Right or wrong?  I have no idea.

For what it's worth, here is the formula of how interest is accrued:

     05  WS-ACCRUAL-DAYS     PIC S9(3)     COMP-3.
     05  ANNUAL-INT-RATE     PIC V9(5)     COMP-3.
     05  RES-INT-FACTOR      PIC V9(7)     COMP-3.


     COMPUTE RES-INT-FACTOR ROUNDED =                        
                            ANNUAL-INT-RATE / WS-ACCRUAL-DAYS
     COMPUTE RES-TODAY-ACC ROUNDED =                         
                            D-RSRV-CURR-BAL * RES-INT-FACTOR 
     ADD RES-TODAY-ACC TO D-RSRV-ACC-INT                     

Essentially, we accrue interest (to three decimal positions) daily on the
balance owing, and we then add today's accrued to the accrued interest up
until today.  I guess one thing that I can't say for sure is "right or
wrong" is should that final ADD have a ROUNDED in it?  Without it we are
simply truncating, which can only be to the customer's benefit, so that
seems OK.

I hope I didn't give away any business secrets.  I don't believe that we are
doing things any different than hundreds of other banks (though I'm sure not
every bank does it this way).

Anyway, if nothing else all of this had pushed me to understand more exactly
how we are doing things.  That never hurts!

Thanks for everyone's input.  It is my hope that this will be the last post
on this (though I wouldn't place any bets!).

Frank
```

---

<a id="t-ae9e9d23b3"></a>
## Thread: Make Sense Of Google Adsense And Make Money!- Lesson 1 — 1 msgs

### Make Sense Of Google Adsense And Make Money!- Lesson 1

- **From:** "arigano.spagety@gmail.com" <arigano.spagety@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T02:42:34
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1191379354.254288.124840@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com>`

```
Make Sense Of Google Adsense And Make Money!- Lesson 1

As a successful web publisher looking to get to the next level, you
are probably thinking about trying a  number of different approaches
to increasing revenue - especially your passive revenue, the money
that  just "shows up" from time to time while you focus on other
things. You may be considering various forms  of internet advertising.
And you should.

Internet advertising has exceeded all expectations. The technology
keeps advancing, and the popularity  and ubiquity of the medium is
incredible. Most companies can benefit from the Internet because it
allows  the chance to reach a global audience at never-before-possible
speeds, with many kinds of filters to make  sure the right audience
sees the right message, and costs that are often surprisingly low. But
many  businesses make money online in ways that have very little to do
with their own actual business, but with  systems that connect various
companies and offerings together.

You may be wondering how to make money easily with online advertising,
and you have come to the right  place!

The secret is that taking ads is a much more profitable strategy then
placing ads for most websites. And  the best way to make money with
this right now is "hiding in plain sight" from the world's most
popular  search engine company.

While there are many ways to approach online advertising, it makes
sense the best would be offered by  the ultimate search engine
company, the company that is setting out to organize all of humanity's
vast  amounts of information - Google. This method is Google Ad Words,
and it's sister program, Google  AdSense. With Ad Words, Google makes
a lot of money. With AdSense, web publishers - that's you! - can
reach a lot of people and make a lot of money too!

In this report, we're going to focus in on how to make sense of, and a
lot of money with, Google's  AdSense program.

Want to make five or ten or twenty thousand dollars a month
automatically and be paid by one of the  biggest Internet companies in
the world?

We'll describe what AdSense is, how it works, how to get started, how
to maximize the program, and  where to go from here! Ready?

First let's examine what AdSense is and how it works.

What is Google AdSense?
Google AdSense is an ad-serving program that places ads that "make
sense" - specifically, that make  sense based on relevant content, and
assumptions about who might be interested in that content.  AdSense is
an application of the broader concept of Contextual Marketing.
Contextual Marketing is just  what it sounds like. On a website about
custom cars, you might have an ad for fancy wheels or car care  kits.
On a website about off-roading, you might have an ad for durable truck
tires or spotlight rigs. A  hockey site might advertise hockey sticks...
and a tennis site, tennis shoes. Contextual Marketing just  means
aligning the ad serving with the context/nature of the website and its
audience, the same way like  items are grouped in a store. And the
"context" can be cut much finer than website level, it can be page
level, article level, and so on, so the ads always match the material
they are appearing near, and thus,  appeal to the interest of the
reader/buyer at any given moment.

So what's in it for you - the website publisher/owner? That's easy.
Every time a visitor to your site clicks  one of these ads, you make
money. The better the ads are targeted, the more clicks you get, the
more  money you make. AdSense displays easy-to-read, text-based,
relevant ads that don't overshadow the content of your website or
annoy visitors. Actually, you have seen hundreds of these ads
yourself, as  you'll realize in a moment.

Today there are probably only a few places in your website that can
directly make you money, if any. The  magic of Google AdSense is this:
It allows you to earn money through every page of your website. On
top  of that, with algorithms used to align ad content with page
content Google automatically selects and displays ads for your website
that are likely to generate the highest revenue for you.

AdSense is an application of AdWords beyond Google.com
Making "sense" of AdSense means understanding that it is really an
offshoot of the older Google model,  AdWords. But they are not the
same thing.

*Google AdWords
If you have ever searched on Google, you have seen AdWords.

Google AdWords are the small text ads that are displayed on the side
of the search results in Google.  These ads are contextual of course -
in this case the context is the search terms you entered. The ads
will  be served based on what you're looking for. So, if you do a
search on "printing services" you will get ads  for printing services
along with your search results!

How does this generate money for Google and the advertisers?

Google AdWords works on a model known as pay-per-click. When someone
clicks on an AdWords ad, the  advertiser of that ad has to pay Google
a certain amount. It's easy to track and fully automated.

We say "certain amount" because that amount is different all the time
and is subject to market forces - in  this case, bidding. Something
has to determine the placement of those ads, and what determines it is
how  much the advertiser offers to pay per click. When you submit this
kind of ad to Google, you submit it with  a bid - which is compared to
other bids for similar keywords. It's an auction style process.

The position of the AdWords ads is based on the bidding amount of
keywords of an ad. Obviously, the  higher the bid, the higher the
position the bid buys you. For instance, if the highest bid for the
keywords  "Internet marketing" is 80 cents per click, you can get top
position by bidding 90 cents. Then every time  someone clicks on your
ad, Google would charge you 90 cents, and you would retain the top
position until  someone else bids more for the same search word
placement.

Google AdSense, as stated earlier, is an extension of Google AdWords.
It takes the AdWords concept and  moves it off of Google's search page
and onto thousands of other web pages. The ads that are displayed  on
the websites are Google AdWords ads.

It's good for the advertiser, who gets more and broader displays, for
Google, who charge more for this  program, for you, who will make
money from the clickthroughs on your pages, and also for the
consumer,  who will see cool ads for stuff they are probably
interested in!

So how exactly are you making money from all this? You earn a share of
the pay-per-click amount, every  time a visitor clicks on the ad. So
you just need to get ads on your site...

How the Ads Get On Your Site
Once you join the AdSense program - and Google approves your site or
sites, which we'll cover in detail  shortly - the actual process of
displaying ads on your website is extremely simple. You are provided
with  a small HTML script, which you can paste anywhere on your
webpage. You may paste this code any  number of times and on multiple
pages, and watch the ads get served! Google automatically places
AdWords ads that are most relevant, from its extensive advertiser
base. Google employs various  sophisticated algorithms to determine
the relevancy of the ads to your content.

Before we get into how Google assesses sites, and whether a few or
many placements of that magic script  will deliver optimum results,
let's review the advantages of AdSense.

*Advantages of the Google AdSense system
Google AdSense is simple to use and manage. Once approved, displaying
ads on your webpage does not  take time and requires minimal technical
knowledge. Google AdSense is very smart about ad selection.  The ads
that are displayed are only those that are relevant to your content,
and Google has strict quality  control on the general quality of the
ads.

Google AdSense uses text-only ads. Far from being low-tech, text ads
are more effective than flashy ads.  The clickthrough rate of text ads
is proven to be higher than that of animated ads! More clicks equals
more money, and text ads equals more clicks. Also...text ads are very
"low weight" and very fast loading.  The AdSense program will not slow
your website, or your visitors, down. You can also use graphic ads.

Google AdSense programs: Choose your weapon(s)!
Google currently (mid 2005) offers three programs: AdSense for
content, AdSense for search, and  Premium AdSense.

*AdSense for content
AdSense for content is the main AdSense program - contextually
targeted ads are displayed on your  webpage, and you get paid for
every click. These ads are specifically targeted to the content of
your  webpage. Consequently, if you change the content of your page,
the ads that are displayed would also  change. AdSense allows you good
control over what ads get served, since there might be some obvious
problems if it didn't. These include:

*Blocking Ads - you choose
While the ad selection is automatic, you can block unwanted ads - such
as those from your competitors.  You just tell Google which companies
to block from your sites.

*Reviewing and filtering Ads - semi-automatic
Google has its own strict policies for ad content (no profanity, no
racial discrimination, etc). These are  filtered out automatically
from your point of view. Additionally, you can choose to block
additional content  through custom filters. This is slightly different
than blocking an advertiser, since with this method an advertiser you
generally allow may submit ads that would individually be filtered
out.

*Allowing the publisher to select an ad
In the rare case where Google's content engines can't make a clear
match of ads to pages, they will let  you pick which ads you want
served. If you don't pick, you'll get public service spots.

The look-and-feel of Ads can be customized
Ads shouldn't look out of place or be jarring on your website. Google
allows you to customize colors and  layout. You can choose options
provided or have your own custom layout and color applied to the ads.
The advertisers have no control over this; as the material appears on
your site, you can and should select  how it looks.

*Tools for analysis
Tracking and analyzing the performance of any marketing campaign is
critical. Google AdSense provides a  range of comprehensive online
reports that allow you to monitor and analyze your earnings as well as
the  performance of ads by many metrics, including size, color, and so
forth, so you can make adjustments to maximize the value of the
placements.

*AdSense for Search
This is another program whereby Google allows you to ad a Google
Search box to your website. By doing  this, visitors to your site can
search the entire internet from your website itself. In a way, your
site  becomes a host to Google. Except you make money!

The search results page that is displayed when a visitor on your
website searches through the search box  on the site itself, also
displays Google Ads (AdWords ads) next to the usual listings, just as
it would on  Google.com. If the visitor clicks on any of these Google
Ads, you get paid as well. Thus, with AdSense for  search you can
enhance your income potential beyond what just the content of your
actual site can  provide.

AdSense for search offers all the same options and controls as AdSense
for content. These include ad  customization, ad filtering, monitoring
and tracking results, and so on.

*Google AdSense Premium service
The AdSense Premium service provides advanced functionality and
features, provided your website  qualifies for it - which in fact very
few sites do.

As a base requirement, your website should received at least 5 million
search queries or 20 million page  views a month to qualify for the
Premium service. (We're betting if your sites qualify for this level
of  service, you already know all about AdSense!)

To Be Continued To Lesson 2 ...

Information:
http://www.Forex.79br.com
```

## Thread: Need some Cobol help — 6 msgs

### Need some Cobol help

- **From:** Howard <how@now.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T14:12:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I'm a VB programmer and I ran into an old dos program written in cobol. 
It uses a number of select statements for printers. Here is an example 
of them.

:STARTFG1
SET PVER=1
XCOMMS 16
PORTCON%PVER% 1 1200 7 1 1 8

or

:GENER
SET PVER=0
XCOMMS 16
PORTCON%PVER% 1 9600 8 0 1 8


Can someone explain this to me, I want to use a laser printer for the 
program. Right now all it see's is dot matrix printers


any help would be appreciated.


Thanks

Howard
```

#### ↳ Re: Need some Cobol help

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T13:32:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13g7o17ho8b9h6b@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Howard wrote:
> I'm a VB programmer and I ran into an old dos program written in
> cobol. It uses a number of select statements for printers. Here is an
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

These are not COBOL commands of any kind, certainly not SELECT statements.

I'm guessing these commands contain initialization strings for a serial port 
(9600 8 0 1...)

Try a "Generic / Text only" printer Windows definition.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

> It uses a number of select statements for printers. Here is an example
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Need some Cobol help

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-10-03T19:12:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CQRMi.274$Pv2.210@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com>, Howard <how@now.com> wrote:
>I'm a VB programmer and I ran into an old dos program written in cobol. 
>It uses a number of select statements for printers. Here is an example 
>of them.

Who told you this was Cobol?
>
>:STARTFG1
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>program. Right now all it see's is dot matrix printers

Those are MS-DOS batch file commands to configure a serial port. Unless your 
laser printer has a serial interface -- most don't -- it isn't going to work.

The strings "1 9600 8 0 1 8" and "1 1200 7 1 1 8" are configuring the serial 
port transmission parameters. Without documentation, it's not possible to say 
with certainty which parameters are which, but the second parameter 
(9600/1200) is certainly the baud rate. The third parameter is probably the 
number of data bits, and the fourth one is probably the number of stop bits. 
I'm guessing that the first parameter indicates parity. No idea at all what 
the 5th and 6th parameters represent.

I imagine that "1 9600 8 0 1 8" means 9600 baud, even parity, 8 data bits, 0 
stop bits, and "1 1200 7 1 1 8" means 1200 baud, even parity, 7 data bits, 1 
stop bit. But don't take that to the bank.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need some Cobol help

- **From:** "billious" <billious_1954@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T07:25:43+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4704250a$0$6023$a82e2bb9@reader.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Howard" <how@now.com> wrote in message 
news:13g7mt02tdv0432@corp.supernews.com...
> I'm a VB programmer and I ran into an old dos program written in cobol. It 
> uses a number of select statements for printers. Here is an example of 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Howard

Worse than any of the above - they're commands that are likely to be in a 
DOS batch file - but for the most part they're not DOS Batch commands, 
they're commands for proprietory executables.

The strings with the leading colons are labels - there should be a "GOTO 
labelname" elsewhere in the full batch.
SET establishes an environment variable
XCOMMS would be a custom executable, doing heaven-knows-what
PORTCON%PVER% would evaluate to PORTCON0 and PORTCON1 for the two frags 
posted. These are likely to be batch files to which the parameters listed 
(and likely correctly analysed by others) are delivered - but may also be an 
executable.

Probably better to post on alt.msdos.batch.nt, but with a far more 
substantial codefrag and be able to supply such info as what XCOMMS and 
PORTCONn are - they would be found as .EXE, .COM, .CMD or .BAT files 
somewhere in the path....
```

```
commenting on "quality" being the ONLY thing remembered a year later and NOT 
whether the project was delivered on time or whether the customers were happy.

It was my experience that a year later, "end users" (or customers) remember:

A) is it working today?
B) how many "fixes" have been needed since it was delivered
C) Was getting it in the first place a "good" experience (i.e. on time and what 
was requested).


Now, if the definition of "quality" (in RW's note)means "is it working correctly 
today" - then I suppose that is true (and first place in MY list) - but I don't 
think I remember cases of "working today" AND NOT "users were happy when 
delivered" AND users actually happy today (because to get from "not happy 
originally" to "working today" means a lot of pain during  the year in-between)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ [OT[ Louder cars (was: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T20:03:56+00:00

I don't know if this made national news yesterday, but I heard a report of some 
visually impaired groups/individuals protesting to get car manufacturers who 
produce hybrid cars to provide NOISIER cars (or some additional noise).  It 
seems that such cars are dangerous for those with sight impairment who rely on 
the noise of oncoming cars - when they are crossing the street as pedestrians.

cross streets safely) I had never thought of this issue before.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T12:06:44-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com> <x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mju3cFdu18oU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Thu, 4 Oct 2007 23:37:30 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
>news:x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> or it was delivered on time. They will judge it on quality.

Let me clarify. Deadlines are too often used as an exuse for poor quality. They say, 'We
don't have time to do it right. It's due today. Just get it done as quickly as possible.'
I respond, 'We'll get it done today AND it will be done right.'

I keep reading that a high percentage of IT projects miss their deadlines. My experience
must be atypical, because I've never worked on a project that missed its deadline. At
least, I can't recall one in the last twenty years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T11:16:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ml71qFdmf1rU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com> <x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mju3cFdu18oU1@mid.individual.net> <fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 4 Oct 2007 23:37:30 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I agree with your sentiments that getting quality AND getting it on time are 
the goals to go for.

The only way I have ever managed to achieve this is by timeboxed, iterative, 
interactive (evolutionary) development.

It CAN be done, but doing so usually requires some major re-thinking in the 
development strategy. It also means establishing enough credibility that 
they will let you depart from their usual approaches :-)

I wish I could share your experience of things being done on time but I 
can't. Certainly, over the last 15 years I have built a good track record of 
delivering good stuff on time, but it is only with timeboxed development and 
a DSDM based approach that this has happened.  I still see other projects 
coming in late and I've had long discussions with other project managers who 
are interested in the methods I use.

(There is a half written book about this sitting on my hard drive; it is one 
of my back-burner projects. Hopefully, I'll finish it next year :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-06T11:33:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mnsdfFe6gk5U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com> <x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mju3cFdu18oU1@mid.individual.net> <fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com> <5ml71qFdmf1rU1@mid.individual.net> <2gjcg39g5f3gpfj50cdd32t6b4jaf0r64k@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:2gjcg39g5f3gpfj50cdd32t6b4jaf0r64k@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 5 Oct 2007 11:16:24 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> tends to be accompanied by interactive (evolutionary) requirements.

That is exactly the point Howard, and I've covered it here so often.

Evolutionary requirements are exactly what makes a system responsive and 
flexible. There is NO problem with continuously changing requirements 
PROVIDED JAD and timeboxing are utilised, and the "customer" KNOWS that 
changing requirements MAY mean some functionality will not be delivered. It 
is my experience that when this is clear, and the final priorities are down 
to the customer, not IT, everything is fine. When IT works WITH peole 
(instead of "us" and "them") it becmes a true joint venture and all 
concerned realise that quarts don't fit into pint pots. What is delievered 
will be what is most important to the people using it, (even though 
priorities and requirements change in the real world) and that is good for 
IT and the Business.

The fundamental concepts are:

1. Don't build everything right away. You are going to change it anyway, so 
build what is perceived as most important NOW. (Use Cases decide the 
functionality, Object modelling decides the components, the Business decides 
the priorities.)

2. Timebox development and iterations.

3. Use JAD workshops to review what is being developed, to prototype user 
interfaces in the context of the Use Case, and to re-prioritise if 
necessary.

Amazing results in terms of useful and timely functionality delivered, and 
satisfaction in both IT and the Business can be achieved with this approach.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-10-08T08:41:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53gkg3d6r2r541stofe1q5084brqo56od3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com> <x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mju3cFdu18oU1@mid.individual.net> <fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com> <5ml71qFdmf1rU1@mid.individual.net> <2gjcg39g5f3gpfj50cdd32t6b4jaf0r64k@4ax.com> <5mnsdfFe6gk5U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 6 Oct 2007 11:33:18 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>1. Don't build everything right away. You are going to change it anyway, so 
>build what is perceived as most important NOW. (Use Cases decide the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>2. Timebox development and iterations.

Evolutionary requirements are very useful - as long as those making
them have the authority to sign off on evolutionary costs - and as
long as the big wigs don't look at the final cost and define the
project as a failure.

We keep hearing about tremendous failures in our industry.   These are
often because huge projects have too much inertia to turn around
quickly to adjust to evolving requirements.   On the other hand, it's
easy to plan on a couple of extra days for a 40 hour project need to
adjust to a different requirement.   (Those days might take a couple
of hours of programming - but requirements changes often need
sign-offs).
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-10T03:17:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5n1gslFfqdv7U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com> <x6%Mi.27132$vo5.18702@fe04.news.easynews.com> <5mju3cFdu18oU1@mid.individual.net> <fu6ag3l4us8vl4bg4uoqvnf0u83b6ebfug@4ax.com> <5ml71qFdmf1rU1@mid.individual.net> <13gltchrkh3g203@corp.supernews.com>`

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13gltchrkh3g203@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
> drive:-)

Ah, Charlie, I have never been one to sow seed on stony ground... :-)

Besides, the total amount I have contributed here would be equivalent to 
several books.

I was going to write a book on OO COBOL (way back when I first got the hang 
of it) but Wilson Price very kindly gave me a copy of his, and it was so 
good I decided I had nothing to add.

I tried to persuade the local Polytechnic to pick it up as a COBOL text, and 
almost succeeded, but in the end they decided to drop COBOL.

The Book that is currently on my hard drive will get written (I had another 
look at it and it is nearer 70% :-)) because it addresses a wider audience 
than just the COBOL community. It is about Project Management rather than 
programming, and has some quite radical ideas which I have found work very 
well in practice. When I was first approached to write it, I was kind of 
reluctant (too busy) and  they wouldn't pay any advance, so I said, "OK, it 
gets done when it gets done..."  I have writing contacts who have POD (Print 
on Demand) publishing facilities and I'll probably hand it to them when its 
done.

I haven't done any writing for ages and I need to get back to it.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T08:01:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80s9g31bfnnv2dasth032mhkpobe11f2js@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <3li8g39823e64hddtjdkm6r6iq8oihjv0a@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 Oct 2007 21:07:55 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>Respondants are short sighted. A year hence, no one will care whether the client was happy
>or it was delivered on time. They will judge it on quality.

"Quality" depends on what was needed and wanted.    All those survey
items were measures of quality.

I remember reading an article about school buildings - the author was
proposing that the big, expensive showcase schools were mistakes. They
were designed to last forever - but didn't adjust well to new
demographics and technology.  

A few years ago hospitals were being designed around all the new bulky
electronics that were now available.   Now the monitors in the
operating room are flat, and the electronics in the patients rooms
roll in and out.

And companies with well designed, well written CoBOL batch programs
are asking to have real-time answers that they can play with on their
handheld computers.    So we have to change our definition of
"quality".

(This is nothing new - people have *always* complained about the new
guys not being interested in quality.   The new people have different
definitions of what quality should be).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** bill@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon)
- **Date:** 2007-10-04T22:48:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ml8ufFdu0njU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <fe1ihd$hes$1@reader1.panix.com> <5mju82Fdq10aU1@mid.individual.net> <5mk5neFdoipaU1@mid.individual.net> <v00ag35g8f9ijbhqqaed5fe9k9tbqcu0hg@4ax.com> <5ml7jdFe5sajU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5ml7jdFe5sajU1@mid.individual.net>,
	"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same question as I asked Bill: 

Somehow I missed where you asked me that, I guess.

>                                Do you have no opinion on what matters most 
> when getting a job done, Howard?

Of course I have an opinion but that survey was more along the lines of:
"Answer Yes or NO, have you stopped beating your wife?"

> 
> I would agree the quiz may be frivolous, but I don't think it's silly. The 
> results surprised me.

The results from a meaningless question where other answers are just
as possible is equally meaningless.  Given the apparent desire to keep
it simple a better way would have been to have people rank those options
as to their importance.  And maybe included a place to write in something
else that the voter considered of greater importance.  And then, use all
that input to create a more meaningful survey.

But that's just my opinion.  :-)

bill
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T11:36:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ml872Fe337qU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net> <4704CB91.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```


"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:4704CB91.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com...
>>>> On 10/3/2007 at 6:19 PM, in message
> <5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net>,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The Code Project is an excellent group of developers in various languages. 
They run these polls every month (I thought it was weekly, but I see I was 
wrong about that). I don't think they take them too seriously and sometimes 
(as in this case) the results can be surprising. There are over half a 
million people in this group, working at the coal face in many and varied 
industries. What they report through these polls is usually very 
interesting. Typically there will be around 4000 respondents, so it is a 
reasonable sample size.

In terms of your dilemma above Frank, they aren't "looking for" anything. It 
is really just a bit of light relief from the trials of cutting code. (I'm 
sure that many here can relate to that :-))

As you can only vote once (I tried doing it twice and they were onto me :-)) 
you can elect either of the scenarios you mentioned above, but not both of 
them :-)

Finally, ANYONE can suggest a poll and they will probably run it. If people 
here think this one is "silly" why not suggest a better one?

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: What matters most when getting a job done?

- **From:** Tom <thasselb@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-10-05T20:35:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1191641745.892687.209380@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5miprnFdk5d6U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Oct 3, 8:19 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> I think many people here will have thoughts on this topic and may want to
> vote in the poll. Be quick, it closes soon...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

What SHOULD matter the most was did the project meet its objectives.
Was the business able to function the way they thought would when the
envisioned the project.  In reality, people seem to lose sight of
this, and concentrate most on whether the project is implemented on
time.  The error free aspect is usually sacrificed for the sake of
timeliness.
```
